validation.comparison_result: Q6 results match: 1 row expected, 1 row actual

Q6.notes: Correct results. Parallel execution working. Missing zone map usage and SIMD vectorization. HDD-inappropriate madvise hints.

Q6.bottleneck: I/O-bound scan with missing zone map pruning and lack of CPU vectorization
Q6.root_cause: Code defines zone map structures but never actually uses them to skip blocks. On HDD with 60M rows, reading all data sequentially. Additionally, filter evaluation is scalar (no SIMD) despite AVX-512 support and compound predicates. MADV_SEQUENTIAL hint inappropriate for potential zone map skipping on HDD.

analysis.overall: Q6 baseline produces correct results in 35.2ms (1.76x slower than DuckDB's 20ms target). Main bottlenecks: (1) Zone maps defined but not used for block pruning - missing ~50-70% I/O reduction on highly selective date range, (2) Scalar filter evaluation without SIMD vectorization, (3) HDD-inappropriate sequential access pattern when zone maps could enable targeted block reads.

performance_optimizations.description: Implement actual zone map usage for block skipping. Code already loads zone map files (lineitem_l_shipdate.zonemap, etc.) but never reads or uses them. Add logic to: (1) mmap and read zone map metadata files, (2) iterate through zone map entries, (3) check if block's [min, max] overlaps with query predicates (date: [8766, 9131), discount: [5, 7], quantity: [0, 2400)), (4) skip entire blocks that don't overlap. Storage design shows lineitem is sorted by l_shipdate with 100K row blocks, so date predicate should prune ~67% of blocks (1994 is 1/3 of 1992-1998 range). Implementation: Load zone maps before scan loop, build list of qualifying block ranges, modify morsel assignment to only process qualifying blocks. On HDD, this is critical to avoid sequential reads of irrelevant data.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 50-70% reduction in scan_filter time (18.9ms → 6-9ms). Date range [1994-01-01, 1995-01-01) represents ~1 year out of ~7 years of data (1992-1998), so zone maps on sorted l_shipdate column should skip ~67% of blocks. Combined with discount and quantity zone maps, expect 70-80% of blocks to be pruned. On HDD, avoiding ~1GB of sequential reads translates to ~10-12ms saved.

performance_optimizations.description: Vectorize the compound predicate evaluation using AVX-512 intrinsics (hardware supports it per workload_analysis.json). Current code evaluates 4 predicates per row in scalar mode. Use _mm512_loadu_epi32/_mm512_loadu_epi64 to load 16 int32s or 8 int64s per iteration, then use _mm512_cmp_epi32_mask/_mm512_cmp_epi64_mask for comparisons, producing bitmasks. Combine masks with bitwise AND for compound predicate. Process 16 rows per iteration instead of 1. Implementation: Inside morsel loop (lines 91-110 in q6.cpp), replace scalar loop with vectorized batches of 16 rows. Handle remainder with scalar tail. Requires aligned loads or use unaligned variants. Note: On HDD system, CPU gains are secondary to I/O, but still valuable - matched rows (1.9% selectivity = 1.14M rows) still require computation. With 64 threads and morsel-driven execution, each thread processes ~937K rows; vectorization reduces filter evaluation from ~937K comparisons to ~59K vector operations per thread.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 20-30% reduction in CPU component of scan_filter (~4-6ms saved on the 18.9ms). SIMD provides 8-16x throughput for comparisons, but memory bandwidth on HDD limits overall gains. Combined with zone maps, total scan_filter could drop to 4-6ms.

performance_optimizations.description: Replace MADV_SEQUENTIAL (line 41) with HDD-appropriate madvise strategy. Current MADV_SEQUENTIAL triggers aggressive read-ahead, which is wasteful if zone maps will skip blocks. For HDD with zone map pruning: (1) Use MADV_RANDOM or no madvise initially to prevent read-ahead, (2) After determining which blocks qualify via zone maps, use posix_fadvise(POSIX_FADV_WILLNEED) on specific byte ranges for qualifying blocks only, (3) For sequential zones, keep MADV_SEQUENTIAL for those specific ranges. This prevents HDD from reading 1GB+ sequentially when only 300-500MB of qualifying blocks are needed. Implementation: After zone map pruning determines qualifying block list, compute byte offsets for each block (block_size * row_size * block_id), call posix_fadvise on each qualifying range to trigger targeted prefetch. On HDD, random I/O is expensive (~100 IOPS), but zone map pruning reduces total I/O volume enough that targeted reads win over sequential scan of everything.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-15% additional I/O reduction beyond zone maps (2-3ms). Prevents HDD from reading irrelevant blocks between qualifying ranges. Most effective when qualifying blocks are clustered (likely for date-sorted data with date range predicate). Conservative estimate since HDD seek overhead partially offsets savings.

performance_optimizations.description: Reorder compound predicate evaluation to put most selective filters first. Current order (line 93-95): shipdate >= && shipdate < && discount >= && discount <= && quantity <. Workload analysis shows estimated selectivities: l_shipdate range: 0.33, l_discount BETWEEN: 0.09, l_quantity <: 0.48. Combined selectivity: 0.33 * 0.09 * 0.48 ≈ 0.014 (1.4%), actual match rate is 1.9% (close). Most selective first: discount (0.09), then shipdate (0.33), then quantity (0.48). This means discount filters out 91% of rows immediately, then shipdate filter runs on only 9% of data instead of 100%. Implementation: Reorder predicate checks to: discount check first, then date range, then quantity. For branch-based short-circuit evaluation, this reduces total comparisons. Note: With SIMD vectorization, predicate order matters less (all evaluated in parallel), but still helps with selection vector compaction.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% reduction in CPU filter overhead (1-2ms). Branch prediction improves when most rows fail the first predicate. Benefit diminishes with SIMD (which evaluates all in parallel), so this is most valuable in scalar tail handling or if SIMD is not implemented.

performance_optimizations.description: Add software prefetching for column data ahead of filter evaluation. Current code accesses 4 columns per row (shipdate, discount, quantity, price) with no prefetch hints. On HDD, cache misses are expensive. Use __builtin_prefetch or _mm_prefetch to hint next morsel's data 1-2 iterations ahead. Implementation: Inside morsel loop, add prefetch for next 16-32 rows: __builtin_prefetch(&shipdate[i+16], 0, 3); __builtin_prefetch(&discount[i+16], 0, 3); etc. This overlaps HDD read latency with computation of current rows. Prefetch distance should be tuned to HDD latency (~10ms seek + 100MB/s transfer = ~0.5ms per 64KB block). For 64 threads on 64 cores, each thread has dedicated CPU cycles for prefetch computation.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% reduction in memory stall cycles (1-2ms). Most effective on HDD where memory latency is high. Diminishing returns if zone maps already reduce I/O to clustered blocks (which have better locality). Conservative estimate since parallel execution on 64 cores reduces per-thread memory pressure.

performance_optimizations.description: Q6 uses Kahan summation (lines 102-106, 124-131) for numerical accuracy on revenue aggregation. While correct, Kahan summation adds 3 extra operations per matched row (y = revenue - comp, t = sum + y, comp = (t - sum) - y). For 1.14M matched rows across 64 threads, this is ~3.4M extra FP ops. Consider: (1) Verify if precision matters for TPC-H Q6 (revenue in dollars, not scientific computing), (2) If validation passes with standard summation, replace with simple += to save ~30% of aggregation arithmetic. Implementation: Replace Kahan summation blocks with simple local_sum += revenue. Test validation to ensure results still match within TPC-H tolerance. If validation fails, revert. Note: This is a risk/reward tradeoff - Kahan prevents accumulation error over 1M+ additions, but TPC-H typically doesn't require that precision (validates to 2 decimal places).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1-2ms reduction in aggregation CPU overhead. Kahan summation is not the bottleneck (only affects matched 1.9% of rows), but eliminating 3 operations per match is measurable. Low impact compared to I/O optimizations.

summary: Q6 iteration 0 produces correct results in 35.2ms (1.76x slower than DuckDB's 20ms target). PRIMARY BOTTLENECK: Zone maps are defined but never actually used - code loads zone map structure definitions but doesn't read metadata files or perform block pruning. This misses ~50-70% I/O reduction on HDD. SECONDARY BOTTLENECK: Scalar filter evaluation without SIMD (AVX-512 available but unused). RECOMMENDED ACTION: (1) Implement zone map block pruning first (highest impact on HDD), (2) Add AVX-512 vectorized filtering, (3) Optimize madvise hints for HDD with zone map access pattern.
